nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, lagoons, and back-reef zones; tolerates a broad range of light and flow conditions

decorPreferences: stable surface — frag plug or rock rubble
space from neighbors to prevent chemical aggression

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding of meaty small foods — micro-plankton, mysis shrimp, or brine shrimp — has been shown to benefit growth and coloration. Feeding in the evening when polyps are expanded is generally recommended.

goodWith: other peaceful LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive sweeper-tentacle corals placed in direct contact
fast-growing SPS corals that may overgrow it
large aggressive LPS (Torch, Hammer, Frogspawn) without adequate spacing

summary: Leptastrea is an encrusting LPS coral considered one of the more beginner-friendly stony corals, tolerating moderate lighting and flow with minimal aggression toward neighbors.
careNotes:
Leptastrea is a genus of massive, encrusting LPS corals in the family Leptastreidae (previously Faviidae, reclassified 2020). The most commonly traded species is Leptastrea purpurea (Ocellated Brain Coral), though other Leptastrea species appear in the trade under the same common name.

Key care notes:

• Growth habit: Leptastrea encrusts across available substrate rather than growing upright. Colonies tend to form low, rounded mounds or flat sheets with tightly packed, small corallites. Captive specimens typically reach 5–30 cm across depending on conditions and time kept.

• Lighting: Moderate PAR (generally cited as 75–200 PAR) is well-tolerated. Leptastrea adapts to a wide range of intensities and is notably less photosensitive than many other LPS. New specimens should be introduced at lower light levels and gradually moved to their preferred position over 1–2 weeks.

• Flow: Moderate, intermittent flow is generally advised — enough to keep detritus from settling on the coral's surface but not so strong as to damage extending polyps. Alternating wavemaker patterns tend to work well.

• Placement: Leptastrea is one of the more placement-flexible LPS corals. It can generally be placed anywhere in the aquarium that meets its moderate light and flow requirements, including the lower rockwork or substrate where PAR is lower. It will encrust onto whatever surface it contacts.

• Aggression: Leptastrea is broadly considered one of the lower-aggression LPS corals and does not produce the long sweeper tentacles characteristic of genera like Hammer or Frogspawn. Some sources (LiveAquaria) list it as 'aggressive' with reference to nighttime sweeper tentacle extension, but hobbyist consensus and broader care guides describe it as relatively peaceful — it has short sweepers that pose minimal risk to neighbors kept at a few centimeters' distance. Maintain a buffer of at least 5–8 cm from neighboring corals as a precaution.

• Feeding: Leptastrea derives the majority of its nutrition from zooxanthellae (photosynthesis). Weekly supplemental target-feeding with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp or micro-plankton in the evening, when polyps are extended, has been reported to support faster growth. This is optional under good lighting conditions.

• Water chemistry: Standard reef parameters are required. LiveAquaria specifies calcium, strontium, magnesium, and iodine supplementation along with trace elements. Ensure alkalinity (kH) remains stable in the 8–12 dKH range.

• Color morphs: Leptastrea is widely bred in captivity and is available in diverse color morphs (orange, green, red, purple, and patterned combinations). Trade names like 'WWC Burning Leptastrea', 'Kermit the Frog', and 'John Deere Leptastrea' refer to named color morphs of the same species.

• Source disagreement on care level: LiveAquaria lists care level as 'Moderate'; World Wide Corals lists it as 'Easy'. The beginner rating used here reflects the broader hobbyist consensus that Leptastrea is among the most forgiving LPS for newer reefers, tolerating imperfect parameters better than most stony corals.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. Because Leptastrea encrusts, frags are typically made by cutting or chiseling pieces from the edge of an established colony and mounting them on frag plugs. Frags readily attach to new surfaces and begin encrusting. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but is not practiced in the home hobby.

additional.notes: Confirmed care level Easy, lighting Medium, flow Medium. 22 Leptastrea products listed as LPS aquacultured corals. No detailed water parameters provided on product pages.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Leptastrea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: family Leptastreidae (reclassified from Faviidae in 2020), order Scleractinia. Seven described species confirmed including L. purpurea as the nominal/most common. Indo-Pacific distribution confirmed. No husbandry parameters.
